The 25 Best Audiophile Headphones Of 2025 - Ultimate List

Lists / Top 10'sNews
Dec 12
Written By Audiophile On

UPDATED: December 2025


Pursuing audio fidelity usually involves a lot of tinkering and making many audiophile headphone purchases and upgrades along the way. 

If you are anything like me, you might start with a set of simple budget audiophile headphones or studio monitors like the gorgeous DT900 Pro X and combine them with a relatively inexpensive DAP (Digital Audio Player). You think you're done… but you definitely aren’t.

Before you know it, you're listening to music on a full array of DACs & tube amplifiers specifically matched for each set of headphones. You may end up owning custom cables and multiple sets of multi-thousand-dollar headphones. What about buying a new MP3 player from our best DAP list?

Your pockets will be significantly lighter, but your music should sound great. That’s the purpose of this best audiophile headphone list: to give you a broad range of options at multiple price points so that you can make an informed choice and get to your perfect set of headphones as quickly as possible.

There is something about this hobby that drags you in. Once it does, it's impossible to go back to using consumer-grade audio equipment. When you own and learn to appreciate your headphones, you truly level up. For me, picking up my favorite headphones is a release, a small vacation in an otherwise hectic day. It feels like one of the few times I can just put down the phone, shut off the laptop, and immerse myself in something that makes me truly happy.

2025 Best Headphone Winner: Focal Utopia

The best audiophile headphones are the Focal Utopia (Click Here for Pricing Info).

I recommend them not only because of their sound quality, which is ultimately the deciding factor, but also because they are packed with technology and premium materials.

They are comfortable for long listening sessions and present sound peerlessly, with natural acoustics and a huge, engaging soundstage. Please read further to see our full list of alternatives at different price points.

Category Specific Recommendations:

  • Best Planar Headphone: Hifiman Susvara - (Check Price)

  • Best Wireless Headphones: Focal Bathys - (Check Price)

  • Best Budget Headphone: Koss Porta Pro Limited Ed - (Check Price)

  • Best Midprice Headphone: Sennheiser HD660S2 - (Check Price)

  • Best Closed-Back Headphones: Sony MDR-Z1R - (Check Price)

Best Audiophile Headphones List 2025

1. Focal Utopia

 
 

Features: Open Back Headphone: Dynamic Driver: Carbon/Metal/Leather Finishing

Pros:

  • Futuristic design and modern materials pushed headphone design forward

  • Endgame-level sound almost perfect for every musical genre

  • Fully immersive

Cons:

  • Expensive

  • Low retail availability - recommended to buy online

Why We Recommend Them

Most headphone companies hope to make waves when releasing new headphones, and Focal managed to create a Tsunami. If you come here wanting to know what headphones have the best sound quality, then the Utopia should be the first place you look if your budget can extend this far.

What makes a headphone great is not simply the sonics. In this end of the market, they need to be special in all areas, from build, design, sound, and performance.

Detail retrieval is jaw-dropping, and the soundstage is massive, providing a speaker-like presentation to the sound.  It is everything I wanted when I got into this hobby, and it feels like an endgame product.

They are light and incredibly well-built, and if I could have one headphone for the rest of the time, it would be the Focal Utopia.

These will be out of reach for most headphone enthusiasts at a shade under four grand. Nonetheless, they are a stunning achievement from the French manufacturer.

Audiophile On Focal Utopia Review (original)

Official Focal headphone website

Buy on Amazon

2. SONY MDR-Z1R

 
 

Features: Closed back earcups: Wired (Dual entry): 70mm Drivers

Pros:

  • Outstanding build quality with kevlar and carbon construction

  • Massive soundstage that rivals many open-back headphones

  • Fully immersive bass

Cons:

  • Very Expensive

  • Limited Availability

Why We Recommend Them

The Sony MDR-Z1R is a surprising take on high-end headphones.  Why surprising? Well, instead of going for the predominantly popular open-back design seen on most top-end headphones, the Z1R is closed-back.  It makes them a whole lot more versatile regarding where and how you use them.

Massive 70mm dynamic drivers (cone-shaped) adorn the internals with sheepskin slathered over the externals. Build quality is top-notch, as they feel premium and solid; there's no need to baby them.

Supremely comfortable, the sound is about big, powerful bass with outstanding detail retrieval. We truly rate them, and the ability to isolate ourselves from the external environment is just the icing on the cake.

Verdict: Best Closed-Back Headphones

Buy on Amazon

3. HIFIMAN SUSVARA

 
 

Features: Open Back Headphone: Stealth Planar Drivers: Dual Entry Cable: 35 Ohm Resistance

Pros:

  • Paired with a quality source, the Susvara is one of the best-sounding headphones ever made

  • Despite the use of metal and wood, they are surprisingly lightweight and comfortable

  • Micro-Detail retrieval is very well done

Cons:

  • At $6000 USD RRP they are prohibitively expensive for many people

Why We Recommend Them

After hearing these fabulous open-back audiophile headphones, you will instantly know why they were added to the list. Hifiman is a colossal audio brand and one of the oldest players in the audiophile game. The Susvara is their best work yet; it is just a stunning all-around headphone that competes with the best of the niche brand headphones we list below.

The Susvara is the result of years of development, which has resulted in an actual reference headphone designed for enthusiasts and developers simultaneously.  They utilize custom driver technology and an open-back housing to bring music that is very true to the source. Based on sound alone, they consistently find themselves on our list of best planar headphones.

Susvaras are constructed using wood and metal. They feel incredible in the hand and on the head. We recommend you use a high-quality source and file to get the most out of them, as they are ruthless in displaying the deficiencies in poorly mastered tracks.

Verdict: Open Back planar headphones with immense detail retrieval.

Buy on Amazon

4. SENNHEISER HD660S2

 
 

Features: Open Back Earcups: Velour Earpads: Dynamic Drivers

Pros:

  • Value for money studio-grade audio quality at a bargain price

  • Iconic styling that is also comfortable

  • Great 660 tuning with elevated bass

Cons:

  • The price increased on the S2 version. The updates justify it but will put it out of reach for some buyers

Why We Recommend Them

These are the long-awaited updates to the excellent value Sennheiser HD660 and Sennheiser kept a lot of the same, but made the tuning even more versatile.

The original HD660 headphones were one of the best-value audiophile-grade headphones on the planet. They were great for critical listening and studio work, but perhaps lacked the range and versatility to sound musical. This iconic headphone is better than ever, and I found myself drawn in with the revised tuning and increased lower frequencies. The bass sounds more impactful, but this also adds an overall weight to its presentation.

Sennheiser also doesn't make unnecessary upgrades and, as such, has a longer product life cycle than other companies that try to catch the flavor of the month vibe by releasing multiple models without significant improvements.

The HD 660S2 is more refined, making it one of the best-sounding Sennheiser headphones of recent years, but it still retains the outgoing model's slightly mid-centric and warmer tones and still retains incredible detail.  They are open-back headphones that pair exceptionally well with tube amps but are driven easily from most high-resolution sources.

Verdict: A reference class open-back headphone. Requires Amplification for best results.

Buy on Amazon

5. Hifiman HE1000

 
 

Features: Open Back: Planar Driver: Cables: Weight - 420g

Pros:

  • Lots of trickle-down technology from more expensive Headphones

  • Iconic design

  • Spacious and rich sounding with lots of micro detail in the midrange

Cons:

  • Exists in a highly competitive price point

  • The suspended headband design will be divisive

Why We Recommend Them

They use different baffle systems from the other Hifiman headphones on this list. This tech sits near the diaphragm, which reduces distortion and provides a purer reproduction of the source material.

Build quality is fantastic, and Hifiman continues to improve and refine its headphone designs to the point that the HE1000 series is one of the best-looking models on the market.

Comfort is also one of the best in class, as it is a very lightweight design paired with beautiful materials at the contact points with your head. The open design of the back allows for both breathability and a wide, airy soundstage.

Verdict: Stunning, transparent-sounding headphones. Big discounts make it a good value.

Buy on Amazon

6. Hifiman Arya

 
 

Features: Open Back: Cable (dual entry): Planar Magnetic Drivers

Pros:

  • Stealthy looks and premium materials

  • Wide headband and large genuine leather earpads

  • Full and immersive sound with big bass

Cons:

  • Large size might be uncomfortable for smaller heads

Why We Recommend Them

If you don't have the funds to shell out over $3000 for outstanding sound, the Arya isn't a cheap set of headphones. However, they are one of the best planar magnetic headphones in 2025 and are well worth the money for many people.

They have a very natural and neutral sound to them. No overemphasis is added to your music, but it is displayed to the fullest. Mids are smooth and highly detailed, and the bass is consistent and punchy while not overbearing.

This is our go-to headphone for soundstage and spacing, displaying live and orchestral music with vivid reality.

The only caveat is that the Hifiman Arya benefits significantly from using a good source. If you do not have one, this will introduce extra expenses to get you to the endgame.

Verdict: With good amplification and source files, this headphone outperforms others in its price category. Fidelity is excellent, as is comfort.

Buy on Amazon

7. MEZE 99 CLASSICs V2

 
 

Features: Closed Back: Mid-Priced Audiophile Headphones: Wooden Earcups

Pros:

  • Excellent value for money

  • Builds on the design of the popular Gen 1

  • Great sound with engaging audiophile tuning that is also adaptable.

Cons:

  • Genuine leather earpads would level them up.

﻿Why We Recommend Them

The original Meze 99 classic headphones were one of my all-time favorites because it just got so many things right, from the design to the tuning, which led them to be a great introduction to audiophile headphones for many people. Few companies other than Audeze, Hifiman, and Focal get their design language over with the community the way Meze has done. You know where a headphone is instantly recognizable at a glance, but the 99 Classic was, and I was delighted to see that it was carried on and refined in the latest iteration.

At first glance, I thought the V2 were just a rebadge, but then I got them in hand for review, and it’s more a case of the little things that add up. The new bass port is being integrated into the jack points, the outward-facing knurling of the wood being more uniform, and the thicker earpads. It’s nice touches like that that make them feel even more premium than ever.

However, the minute you take off the pads and reveal the baffle, you see what this update is all about. The structure of the baffle and housing is completely different than the simple one on the originals. Meze is known for its intricate architecture on its flagship emperyean, and it seems a lot of that ethos has trickled down into their entry-level audiophile headphone, and it really shows in the sound. It’s a night and day upgrade, lots more clarity, lots more detail, more spacing, and more soundstage hits you right off the bat. The sound is more technical and something capable of technical listening duty; it’s fantastic. However, what if you were a fan of the original tuning? Have we lost the warmth and engaging characteristics that made it so popular? Well, no, as it happens, the company included a foam insert that goes behind the pads and makes the sound more natural, warmer, and more acoustic than without.

To me, it felt like I had two headphones in one, something versatile, comfortable, and stylish. This may look like the first release in a lot of ways, but Meze went all out in crafting a new sound while keeping the iconic design.

Buy on Amazon

8. Focal Bathys

 
 

Features: Close Back Headphones: Noise Cancelling Technology: Wireless via Bluetooth

Pros:

  • A luxury headphone with excellent noise cancelation technology

  • Iconic Focal Styling

  • The best-sounding ANC headphone on the market

Cons:

  • More expensive than Sony and Bose variants of this technology

﻿Why We Recommend Them.

The brand is iconic, and I love that they have carried over elements of their house design and sound into what is essentially a day-to-day and travel headphone.  If you want to stand out from the crowd and step away from the mainstream into luxury, this is the one to go for. It's very unlikely you will bump into someone else sporting a pair.

Detailed, transparent, and unlike other wireless headphones, they also have solid mid-bass clarity, adding an extra dimension to the sound.  If comfort is your thing, look no further—The large leather pads and perfect clamping pressure make them easy to listen to all day.

It’s certainly more expensive than other headphones in the category. Still, the difference in quality is there to see at first use, and the sound is representative of what Focal offers in their wired headphones.

Official Focal Bathys Website

Verdict: Hands down the best sounding wireless audiophile headphone with noise cancelling technology (ANC)

Buy on Amazon

9. Sennheiser HD800s

 
 

Features: Open Back: Dynamic Driver: Wired: Reference Headphones

Pros:

  • True reference grade sound, perfect for monitoring, studios and audiophiles

  • Large and spacious earcups reduce fatigue

  • Legendary pedigree descended from the original HD800

Cons:

  • Some listeners consider forward treble too much

Why We Recommend Them

The HD800 was for a long time considered the best audiophile headphone.

The sound is very pure and unadulterated. Sennheiser designed the headphones to recreate the audio being input faithfully; in that regard, they are one of the best at transparency.

Unlike the 820 models listed below, this headphone still uses an open-back construction and continues the tradition of highly detailed yet airy open sound with a massive soundstage.

It's a true classic headphone that is a worthy successor to the original.

The official website for the Sennheiser HD800s

Sennheiser HD800s Review

Verdict: Pure and Transparent with a great soundstage. Visceral, it requires good music files for this headphone to sound best.

Buy on Amazon

10. Sennheiser HD820

 
 

Features: Closed Back: Wired: Dynamic Drivers: Reflective audio technology

Pros:

  • Similar to the HD800s but with the benefit of a closed-back design.

  • The display window to the driver is super cool.

  • Punchier and deeper extension in the low end than Sennheisers open back headphones

Cons:

  • The price is very high, putting it in direct competition with many high-end planar headphones

Why We Recommend Them

The latest development of the Sennheiser HD series of headphones is now their best and most expensive headphones (other than the Orpheus). It is a masterpiece with outstanding resolution and transparency.

I think, however, many people are getting mistaken as to the performance of the HD820. These are not a 1-to-1 replacement for the original HD800 headphones. The HD800 is visceral and cutting. They are ruthless and add minimal flavor to the sound.

The HD820 sounds less like a tool headphone designed for the studio and more like an engaging and enthralling experience.

The HD820 is a headphone dedicated to listening with maximum pleasure.

Audiophile On Sennheiser HD820 Review

Verdict: A super cool headphone with a futuristic design. The sound is perfect for someone who wants a closed headphone with crystal clear sound. Requires amplification.

Buy on Amazon

11. Meze Audio Poet

 
 

Features: Open Back: Compact Planar Driver Headphone

Pros:

  • Empyrean-like tuning and tonality.

  • Excellent technical performance

  • Excellent build quality and finishing worthy of a higher price

Cons:

  • They are still very expensive for a non flagship headphone

Why We Recommend Them

Meze has built itself from entry-level contenders to big-name players in a little over a decade. The growth has been impressive, but more than that, it’s the direction and approach they take to meeting the needs of audiophiles. We all know their flagship Empyrean headphones are one of the best in the game, a stunning amalgamation of design and sound, but for many, they are too expensive and too big. Meze seemed to listen and, with that, recently released the Meze Poet, a more compact planar headphone that has many qualities of its more expensive brother.

The design is simply stunning, with the CNC-milled external shells, leather headrest, and magnetically detachable earpads all screaming luxury. But it’s the sound that makes you sit up and take notice, they developed their own unique driver with a custom ribbon pattern that makes it one of the most versatile headphones on the market, capable of handling any genre and blowing out minute details with jaw-dropping levels of separation.

Despite its high price you cannot look past the poet because it still delivers great value when compared to more expensive offerings in their own lineup, a soulful and beautiful headphone if ever there was one.

Buy on Amazon

12. Audeze LCD-5

 
 

Features: Open Back: Planar Driver: Leather, Metal and Wood Construction

Pros:

  • Pure luxury and excess. High-end materials like wood, carbon, and leather give an unmatched feel.

  • Magnificent at resolving micro details in a complicated music

  • Bass hits hard but doesn’t degrade when the volume is high

Cons:

  • Requires high-quality components like DACs and Amps to drive them to their full potential

  • Expensive

Why We Recommend Them

The top-of-the-line Audeze LCD-5 is a true luxury headphone in every sense of the word and the new flagship headphone from standout brand Audeze.

You are in for a treat with this set of planar magnetic beasts, from the exquisite and ornate packaging to the stellar build.

Made from some of the finest materials, swathed in wood and leather, they look as good as they sound.

Transparent, detailed, fast, and with an excellent sound stage is like having an entire 2-way speaker system planted right on your head.

Official Product page for the Audeze LCD-5

Verdict: A true endgame headphone with flagship-defining sound quality that will make true audiophiles smile from ear to ear. Photos don’t do justice to how good they look in real life.

Buy on Amazon

13. Audeze LCD-2

 
 

Features: Open Back: Planar: Wired: Reissue of Original model

Pros:

  • One of the best headphone reissues of all time

  • Engaging, dynamic sound

  • Analog sounding

Cons:

  • Audeze design is bulky on small heads

Why We Recommend Them

For years, the LCD-2 was one of the most beloved headphones in audiophile forums. This would have been an end-game headphone for many before the market moved north to the uber-expensive territory.

The sound, though, was undoubtedly brilliant. It mixed a natural and airy presentation with a crazy amount of detail and introduced thousands of audiophiles to the world of planar magnetic technology (this tech has since become one of the most dominant in the industry).

Build quality is excellent, and I love that it brings back the classic styling of the original rather than Audeze having tried to modernize everything with new materials. Pair these headphones with a tube amplifier and watch them shine, producing a wonderful analog sound.

Verdict: A legendary headphone that sounds as good now as it did back in the day. A purists headphone that is very good value for money.

Buy on Amazon

14. FOSTEX TH900 MK2

 
 

Features: Closed Back: Dynamic Driver: Wired: Requires Amplifier

Pros:

  • Beautiful construction from Japanese cherry birch

  • Reference grade professional sound

  • Tesla Coil technology

  • Excellent Isolation

Cons:

  • Very power hungry and requires a powerful solid-state amplifier for the best sound. Tube adds too much warmth.

Why We Recommend Them

Another legendary audiophile headphone on this list is the Fostex TH900 Mk2. I consider this an excellent closed-back headphone with a huge open soundstage and stunning low-end performance. The dynamic driver is assisted via Fostex’s well-regarded Tesla technology, which aids in moving the sizeable dynamic driver diaphragms.

I recommend pairing them with a powerful linear/neutral-sounding amplifier like the Sennheiser HDV 820 reference amp for the best sound quality.

The build quality is some of the best, and the stunning wood earcups give a premium feel to this classic design. They are available in many different colors, and using real Japanese Cherry Birch means they all have different grain patterns, so no two headphones will look exactly the same.

One final point is that out of all the audiophile headphones I have tested, the Fostez TH900 MK2 has the highest isolation level. This makes them the best option for people who don’t want to be distracted by their surroundings or are worried about sound leaking on open-back headphones.

Audiophile On Fostex TH900 Mk2 Review﻿

Verdict: The best-isolating audiophile headphone with the deepest bass response while retaining adequate speed for layering in the mid-bass. Brilliant.

Buy on Amazon

15. Focal Stellia

 
 

Features:  Close back: Wired: Dynamic Driver: Easy to Drive

Pros:

  • Sounds like an open headphone

  • Not overly analytical - Designed to enhance music

  • Build Quality and Styling are unrivaled

Cons:

  • Too expensive. HD820 and TH900 rival them on performance for a fraction of the price.

Why We Recommend Them

The Focal Stellia sounds as good as it looks. It’s the closed-back rival to the company’s own Utopia and delivers stunning technical performance and goes a long way to creating an open-back presentation.

Drawing inspiration from the Utopias Aesthetic, the Stellia are gorgeous-looking headphones. In recent years, they have been responsible for modernizing the look of audiophile headphones, which for some time had become stagnant. This feels fresh, bold and exciting.

Focal created a unique internal waffle design on the earcups to aid tuning and reduce sonic reflection to achieve exceptional sound quality that closely mimics an open-back presentation.

Premium materials, as always, are cones to catch the eye and are suitable for use as a portable set if you wish.

Audiophile On Focal Stellia Review﻿

Verdict: The most technically perfect closed-back headphones on the market. Buy this if you want open-back sounding headphones in a closed-back body.

Buy on Amazon

16. Beyerdynamic T1 - 3rd Gen

 
 

Features: Open Back: Dynamic Driver: Dual entry cable: Velour Earpads

Pros:

  • Professional music production-level audio

  • Airy and immersive with a big soundstage

  • Built to last forever

Cons:

  • The design isn’t as high-end as other headphones. Instead, Beyerdynamic has produced premium studio headphones. No wood and leather here.

Why We Recommend Them

Like the Sennheiser HD800, these Beyerdynamic headphones have been around for years for a good reason. Now though, there is a third generation available to buy. If a company produces a golden ears headphones, then it is always best to refine the design instead of coming up with something completely new. That's what is done here, and by gosh, they are special.

The sound is comparable to that of the Sennheiser HD800 but with a bit more of a low-end grunt and warmth, leading to a more natural and less sharp listening experience. It creates a huge open soundstage aided by crystalline floating highs.

Detail retrieval is at an all-time high, thanks to Beyerdynamic's Tesla Driver Technology.  Build quality is top-of-the-line; these headphones should serve you well for decades.﻿

Verdict: Don’t let professional studio styling fool you. These headphones are one of the best, with tight control and lots of definition.

Buy on Amazon

17. Hifiman Ananda Nano

 
 

Features: Open-Back: Nanometer thick drivers : Acoustic Invisible Magnet Tech

Pros:

  • Direct competitor for the Sennheiser HD660S2

  • Ultra light and thin driver tech displays immense detail

  • Easily driven from smartphones

Cons:

  • Not the best for portable use due to open back design leaking sound.

  • Extended highs while not sibilant may not best suit western ears

Why We Recommend Them

The Ananda was a great set of headphones but the new Nano takes things to another level. Hifiman, of course, is a leader in planar magnetic headphone technologies, and with the nano, they have made an ultra-thin lightweight diaphragm that works in combo with their new acoustically invisible magnets to create amazing sound for under $1000.

The soundstage is huge and the imaging unreal. The placement of vocals and instruments is immersive, and the overall clarity of the set has continued to impress us as we dive deeper into testing.

They are a more purist headphone that is set to take on the new Sennheiser HD660S2 and do so by creating an open and light sound. The highs are extended, and they work great at creating a large soundstage. If you aren't used to extending treble clarity, it may be a bit jarring at first, but give it a few hours, and your brain should acclimatize, and you will be rewarded in kind.

Official Hifiman Website﻿

Verdict: The best-sounding wireless headphones. It has audiophile-grade sound with no compromises. Don’t buy if you want a portable headphone due to sound leak.

Buy on Amazon

18. Hifiman HE400SE

 
 

Features: Affordable: Open Back: Planar Driver: Wired

Pros:

  • Low price but the good sound makes them a perfect budget intro to planar headphones

  • Comfortable with good padding

  • Warm, detailed sound

Cons:

  • Lacks technical performance of more expensive models

  • More plastic is used to keep price down

Why We Recommend Them

While there are better sounding, better looking, and all-around better headphones on this list, most also cost far more than the humble Hifiman HE400SE. This is an entry-level audiophile headphone for people on a budget or those just wanting to experiment with planar drivers.

I wanted to include it as an excellent option because, just a few years the thought of getting this type of technology at such a low price was unheard of. By using slightly older tech and making cost savings in the right areas, Hifiman delivers one of the best-sounding cheap headphones on the list. 

A warm-sounding but very detailed Planar headphone weighs only 350g, so they can be worn all day and built to last for years.

Verdict: The best headphone to buy if you are on a budget or want to experiment with this driver type.

Buy on Amazon

19. Koss Porta Pro

 
 

Features: Dynamic Driver: Open Back: Over-Ear: Foldable: Inline Remote

Pros:

  • One of the most iconic headphones of all time

  • Foldable headband for portability

  • Ultra clear distortion-free sound

  • Extremely affordable

Cons:

  • The headband will tear your hair out

  • Cable is a bit flimsy

Why We Recommend Them

The Koss Porta Pro has been on the market for over 30 years but remains immensely popular amongst audiophiles. We have tested and recommended it for years, and we can’t believe how good they sound every time. Koss made a headphone many years ago that others still can’t compete with today.

I love the iconic design and how it has remained relatively unchanged for the duration of its existence (there are now a few color options). It’s recognizable in a way that will get you the nod of approval from people in the know.

I also love that it is so portable. The headband folds up, making a small package that can be thrown in a bag and taken wherever you go.

The sound is very clean and spacious, with beautiful performance and mid-range imaging. It’s lighter on bass, so it's not as suited for rap/EDM, etc, but it works wonders with rock, acoustic, and classical music.

Verdict: The most stylish and best-value headphones in 2025. Also the best model for an audiophile looking for portable headphones.

Buy on Amazon

20. Bang & Olufsen h100

 
 

Features: Luxurious materials, State of the art sound engineering, eye watering price tag.

Pros:

  • By far the most luxurious wireless headphone

  • Detailed and natural sound with good bass. It’s refined and nuance tuned for audiophiles and not in the way generic consumer headphones are tuned.

  • Materials and finishing are class-leading

Cons:

  • Very limited availability

  • Extremely expensive. you are paying for the best wireless headphones but you are also paying a lot.

Why We Recommend Them

Im just finishing up the review of the Bang & Olufsen H100, and kind of knew that they had a spot waiting for themselves here on the Audiophile headphone list. A few people will find that divisive because how do you really justify the cost? Well, the truth is, of course, that not everyone will be able to, but does that mean they aren’t deserving of a mention? In my opinion, this list was never about value. It was about the best headphones, and that is measured regardless of price.

The H100 is what happens when one of the world’s best audio companies takes its might and throws every single bit of knowledge it has at a solution. No expense is spared here, you know, as soon as you pick them up, this is not your ordinary headphone.

In my time using them, I have just been left more and more impressed, wireless and bluetooth never used to signal such an enjoyable listening experience. It was a practical addition, but when you listen to the H100 with rock, classical, and country genres, they just sound so natural and engaging, with hints of warmth and texture. Its a listening headphone trhough and through with a purity to it that wont challenge the studio headphones or the planars but stands in that other category. They sit back in a lounge in an airport, drink in hand, and just drift off into their own little musical bubble. I found it composed and a headphone that I reached for over others from a pure love of the sound standpoint. Does that make it the purest? The most technically advanced? No, but this is a headphone that can do daily life, looks great, and is built like a tank.

They are luxuriously finished in the highest materials. The comfort is on another lever, and the accessories all matched and on point.

Verdict: A luxury headphone that performs strongly.

B&O H100 Review: Flagship, Luxury and Sound combine

Buy on Amazon

21. Focal Clear MG

 
 

Features: Open Back: Dynamic Magnesium Driver: Dual Entry Cable

Pros:

  • New driver tech has dramatically improved midrange performance

  • Comfortable microfibre pads

  • Really good looking headphones

Cons:

  • New materials have increased weight over previous models

  • Expensive (but kind of worth it)

Why We Recommend Them

Focal again has proven that it is best to build upon a successful design instead of creating a new one. The Focal Clear MG is the update of the original Clear model we had such high praise for upon its release.

The MG is an important designation in its name for magnesium. Magnesium is incorporated into the design of the new M-shaped drivers and offers a host of benefits that is very noticeable in how the headphone presents its sound.

Magnesium is both strong and light and has a very low amount of distortion to produce cleaner sound.

AudiophileOn'sFocal Clear Review 

Buy on Amazon

22. Hifiman Shangri-La

 
 

Features: Nanothin driver: Open Back: Amplifier included (Not Sold Separately)

Pros:

  • The kitchen sink of audiophile headphones

  • Sounds like full-size speakers

  • An experiment in how far sound can be pushed from a headphone

Cons:

  • The cost is over $40,000

  • Extremely rare

  • Very Absurd to spend this much on headphones

Why We Recommend Them

I don’t recommend them, at least not to buy. However, if you ever get to listen to them, jump on it because it’s one hell of an experience.

I got 30 minutes of head time in a custom listening booth at High End in Munich in 2019, and my jaw hit the floor. I was skeptical going in but coming out; I had to agree that nothing I had heard previously sounded like this.

It was as close to experiencing speaker-like sound in a room without speakers. It’s an immersive listening experience that wraps you in sound, but it’s not worth purchasing.

The difference between this and a Utopia, Hifiman, or Timsock is very little when discussing technicals. Yes, it presents like a speaker, but I think I could get some tasty speakers instead for that price.

Verdict: The Shangri-La is an experience for sure, but you would have to be mad or a billionaire actually to buy this headphone

View Hifiman Shangri La Details @ Hifiman.com

23. Edifier Stax Spirit S3

 
 

Features: Planar Driver: Wireless: ANC: APTX and High Resolution codec support

Pros:

  • Wireless Planar Headphones

  • Strong Noise Cancelation

  • Solid bass response

Cons:

  • Could do with some material upgrades to take them to the next level

Why We Recommend Them

The Edifier Stax Spirit S3 headphones stand out in the crowded market of high-fidelity audio devices for several compelling reasons. Firstly, their sound quality is exceptional, delivering crisp, clear highs, well-defined mids, and deep, rich bass.

Secondly, the build quality of the Stax Spirit S3 is noteworthy. They are designed with durability and comfort, featuring premium materials that ensure longevity while being comfortable enough for extended listening sessions. The earcups are well-cushioned, and the headband adjusts easily to fit different head sizes, reducing listener fatigue.

Then you find out that this Planar headphone is wireless and has some strong noise cancelation that isn’t far off Bose and Sony.

If you are an audiophile who travels and is looking for wireless headphones (and the Focal Bathys are just too expensive), The Edifier Stax Spirit S3 is our recommendation.

Verdict: An excellent performing headphone for the money. Planar in wireless form.

Buy on Amazon

24. Grado Hemp

 
 

Features: Hemp and Maple consturction, Hand Assembled, 44mm dynamic driver

Pros:

  • By far Grados most well rounded headphone

  • Classic design

  • Beautiful bass response

Cons:

  • It’s expensive for a Grado, but then no other Grado sounds this smooth

  • Cable is on the heavy side

Grado was my first audiophile headphone way back in the 90’s when I scraped together my allowance to buy a pizza box set of SR80, Alessandro MS1s were next up, and it’s a love affair that has continued with the 325s and X versions finding their way into my rotation over the years. However, no Grado headphone has ever hit me in the feels the way the Hemp model has. This is a Grado, and it looks like a Grado but funkier and sounds like a Grado but funkier. Catch my drift?

Grado, of course, are know for their open back design, great mids, and light bass. When I was testing the Hemp thats what I expected, but instead I got a buttery smoothness, a natural purity, a weightier dispersion on the notes and bass. That’s right, bass on a Grado headphone. It’s glorious because you still have the airy and open mids, but everything sounds more impactful and immersive. I found myself pulled into the track and bumping along, being less analytical in my sessions and just grooving. Within a few hours, I was hooked and throwing tracks that I never thought would perform well at them and finding myself with a ridiculous grin on my face.

Now to the technical side, the build is on par with other Grado wood headphone designs, but the grain of the maple stands out a lot. The pads that come with it are, as always, hit and miss, but there are plenty of aftermarket options available to modify the tuning and comfort of the headphone. The price, I think, is spot on, and given there is nothing else out there quite like it, I feel it’s great value for money, but I will mention it’s worth having a good quality source and high quality music files to use with it to get the most from them.

Buy on Amazon

25. Meze Audio 109 Pro

 
 

Features: Dynamic Driver (50mm), Low 40 Ohm impedance and a self adjusting headband

Pros:

  • Stunning good looks are an evolution of the popular 99 Classic model

  • Low impedance makes them easy to drive

  • Exceptional sounding with classical, concert and analog music

Cons:

  • Requires significan burn in (100 hours) to open up to full potential

  • If you have a very small head they look kind of funny because the bad wont expand and leave the headband sticking up

Meze Audio goes from strength to strength. It’s almost a decade since I first reviewed the company and in that time they have been very deliberate to make their new models count. This also means that they produce headphones that stand the test of time in a way like Sennheiser and Beyer do without needing to pump out yearly meaningless updates to stay relevant.

The Meze Audio 109 Pro is positioned price-wise as their mid-tier headphones but for many, this will be worthy endgame stuff set to do battle with the Hifiman and HD660S2 I talk mentioned above. For me, the gorgeous design and all-day wearability give them the edge. I found them so comfortable due to the lightweight framing and relatively small driver. I also found them easy to drive from any source due to the low impedance rating of the headphones.

When I tested the sound it stood out in the presentation of anything tonally natural. I mean that in terms of acoustic guitars, stringed instruments and music that is generally analog vs digital. They retrieve lots of detail and push the sound out with an open and airy soundstage.

Buy on Amazon

Readers Headphone Choice

 
 

A few years ago, we would have put together a list like this and still had plenty of room to spare; however, nowadays, there is so much choice on the market we have to leave a lot off this list of the best audiophile headphones.  

The final spot, as usual, is reserved for the reader's pick; what headphones should we have included? What have we missed out on? What should get recognition?

Honorable Mentions

Plenty of headphones come through our doors that we get to listen to but do not quite have time to do a full review. Some of the ones that impressed us this year have been the:

  • Meze Audio Elite

  • Meze Audio Liric

  • Grado Hemp Headphones

  • Audeze CRBN

These headphones are all currently under review and should be making an appearance on the list this year.

Headphone Buyers Guide and FAQ

Why do people spend so much money on Audiophile products?

Much of this obsession stems from audiophiles (and those who appreciate fidelity in general) having a deeper understanding of how the physical hardware they use affects the reproduction of their music.  It is easy to become obsessed with minor annoyances in recordings, which are often overlooked by a layperson.

As your ability to listen improves, you will soon pick up on barely audible treble spikes, minor bass roll-offs, and much more. This is because when you know what to look for, it's incredible how much of an effect these little things can have on your enjoyment of the music. 

Of course, good music sounds better through good audio equipment, but it is no longer necessary for pleasure.

An extra benefit to purchasing a set of audiophile headphones is that you can have incredible sound, but you don't have to step into the ultra-pricey world of high-end HiFi systems. 

Many people do not have the space to accommodate a full-size Hifi setup, so headphones become a suitable compromise.  By going the headphone route, you can still experience extremely high levels of fidelity in a relatively compact package.

Additionally, the University of Houston had an interesting study that showed that the purchase of luxury goods is tied to the experience. In the headphone world, this is especially true because one is not simply purchasing an inert product but rather one that elicits emotion and experience. Therefore, its value isn’t just tied to the object but to the value the buyer attaches to the way the music sounds.

Will I notice A Difference if I use audiophile Headphones?

A big concern of casual listeners (or people whose listening experiences have, in the past, been limited to consumer-grade headphones) is whether or not they will be able to notice the increased sound quality if they buy a set of audiophile headphones. The answer is most certainly YES! However, you might not be able to articulate what improvements you’re hearing; they simply sound better.

This short article dives a bit deeper into the advantages of upgrading to high-resolution headphones and how you can better learn to understand the causes of increased fidelity.

Things to consider when buying Audiophile Headphones

While sound quality should be of the utmost importance when buying audiophile headphones, don't forget to consider a few other factors when looking for the best solution.  This will further your enjoyment of your headphones in the long term.

The best audiophile headphones in the world might have the best sound, but buying something better suited to the task is probably worth buying if they are too impractical for use in scenarios where you listen to music. We have highlighted some areas below that we think you should consider before purchasing.

Additionally, Audiophile headphones are more about purity, but there is a case to argue that there are headphones that make music sound amazing. This is actually considered different than a true audiophile experience, but many may actually prefer the musical aspects of such headphones.

Comfort & Fit

Comfort and fit both play significant roles in determining how good a match certain headphones are for you.  A set of uncomfortable headphones will make you want not to use them.

On the other hand, good audiophile headphones will be comfortable for long listening periods. Consider how these headphones are designed to be worn and how the materials, ventilation, padding, and weight impact comfort.

Consider Your Budget - The Best Headphones Ain't Cheap

The price is one of the most significant barriers to entry for some of the world's best-sounding headphones. Don't be too disheartened; several budget headphone options produce about 95% of the sound that the multi $1000 versions do. 

The difference between an entry-level audiophile headphone and an ultra-high-end model is a sonic evolution instead of a revolution.

This is something that audiophiles refer to as diminishing returns. A headphone that is twice the price of another rarely will indicate a doubling in sound quality. Nevertheless, some bargains offer much value to headphone buyers on a budget.

What source should I use for high-resolution audiophile headphones?

Music is and always will be the show's star, but your headphones have a massive effect on sound quality. Of course, the same is true for the source you use.  Assuming you are using high-resolution audio files, you will want to use an excellent source to ensure the signal is crispy clean and has enough power to drive your headphones to their full potential.

I use 2 "sources" (listed above) for most of my listening, One for home and one for on-the-go.  My home setup is an insane all-in-one Amp and DAC, and on the go, I will most likely be using my phone with an external DAC or a stand-alone DAP.

However, you don't have to spend much to get great sound. Something like the budget-friendly Objective 2 amp, with its transparency and detail for home use, will blow your mind. On the other hand, if the portable clean sound on a budget is your thing, then many frugal audiophiles still swear by the Clip Mp3 Player by Sansa.

The short version of what I am trying to say is that you upgrade your files first, then headphones, and you can throw any extra at the sources.  The differences in sound quality from changing headphones can be night and day.

To read more about the best source hardware for your music, you can check our list of the best high-resolution music players.

Audiophile Headphone Availability and Servicing

Generally speaking, audiophile headphones will not be cheap, and as with all electronic goods, things can and do go wrong. However, given the high initial cost, most people will want to have a warranty and support system in place.

I tend to refer people to Amazon as a first-stop shop because they are very consumer-friendly when issues arise. However, many companies on this list value their customers and put effort into aftercare.

If it is a foreign company and you need to ship them back to the factory, you will most likely be paying the postage, and you will also want to have them insured both ways, which can certainly add to the cost, worth considering.

Are Audiophile Headphones Better Than Speakers?

No. That’s the simple answer when we are discussing sound quality. A good set of speakers, in our experience, will always beat a great set of headphones. Speakers can take advantage of the space and the more realistic way we listen to music. Sound quality is better on speakers than on headphones.

However, the honest answer is that speakers may work better for you if you don’t have space or the budget for audiophile speakers. In that case, headphones are a fantastic solution that keeps improving yearly.

What audiophile headphones do I use?

One of the most common questions I get asked is: What audiophile headphones do I use personally? As someone who runs a site dedicated to high-end audio products, this comes up a lot, but take these with a grain of salt.

Currently, im using the Focal Utopia I list in the number one position of our 2025 best audiophile headphone list and sometimes switch between the Sennheiser HD800 and B&O H100 depending on what im doing. However, in my daily life, I also regularly use the Apple Airpods Pro 3, having recently upgraded from the 2’s. They are my kind of do it everything, travel, and take calls earphones. Then I use the Shokz Openrun for cycling. I guess what im trying to say is you need to find the right tool for the job.

What works for you might be completely different; there is no right or wrong answer. Also, remember that as the writer for an audiophile website, I will likely change my equipment multiple times a year.
